hints: In English the standard word order is Subject + Verb + Object (SVO).
The subject `She` is already given; the verb must come before the object.

a1.books reads: Wrong order: the object cannot precede the verb in a standard declarative sentence.
a1.reads books: Correct: verb + object follows the SVO pattern.
a1.books she reads: Redundant subject and inverted order; SVO requires subject → verb → object.

hints: SVO order: subject (They) + verb (drink) + object (milk).
After the subject `They`, the verb comes first, then the object.

a2.milk drink: The object cannot come before the verb in a declarative sentence.
a2.drink milk: Correct: verb + object in SVO order.
a2.milk they drink: Redundant subject and wrong word order.

hints: SVO: the verb `eats` must precede the object `lunch`.
After `He`, the verb comes directly, followed by the object.

a3.lunch eats: Object before verb is incorrect in standard English.
a3.lunch he eats: Redundant subject; standard SVO doesn't place the object first.

hints: Standard SVO: subject + verb + object.
After `I`, the verb `watch` precedes the object `TV`.

a4.TV I watch: Disrupted word order with redundant subject.

hints: SVO: We (subject) + play (verb) + football (object).
The verb `play` must come before its object `football`.
Negation with do/does: does + not + main verb + object.
The word `not` goes right after the auxiliary `does`.

a6.does watch not TV: `not` must go between the auxiliary and the main verb.

hints: Negation in Present Simple with `does`: does + not + base verb.
Place `not` immediately after the auxiliary `does`.

a7.does drink not coffee: `not` belongs between the auxiliary and the main verb.

hints: Negation with `do`: do + not + main verb + object.
After `do`, `not` comes immediately, then the verb `play`.

a8.do play not games: `not` must be between the auxiliary and the main verb.

hints: Modal verb + base form: can + speak + object.
After the modal `can`, the main verb in base form comes next.

a9.speak can English: The main verb cannot precede the modal.
a9.can English speak: The object cannot come between the modal and the main verb.

hints: Modal + base verb + object: can + ride + a bike.
After `can`, the base verb `ride` comes next, then the object.

a10.ride can a bike: The main verb cannot precede the modal.
a10.can a bike ride: The object cannot separate the modal from the verb.

hints: SVO: Tom + washes + the dishes.
After the subject `Tom`, the verb comes before the object.

a11.dishes washes the: Completely wrong order with separated article.

hints: SVO: verb `brushes` before object `her teeth`.
After the subject, the verb precedes the object.
Yes/no questions: auxiliary (Do/Does) + subject + main verb.
To form a question with `like`, use: Do + you + like.

a13.You do: This is declarative order; questions need auxiliary before subject.
a13.Do you: Correct: auxiliary inversion for question formation.
a13.Like you do: The main verb cannot start a yes/no question.

hints: Questions with `does`: Does + subject + base verb.
For third person singular, `Does` goes at the start of the question.

a14.She does: Declarative order; `Does` must precede the subject in questions.
a14.Does she: Correct: Does + she — question word order.
a14.Have she does: Wrong auxiliary and completely disrupted order.

hints: SVO: subject + verb + object.
After `The baby`, the verb `drinks` precedes the object `water`.
Modal + base verb + object: can + use + computers.
After `can`, the base verb `use` comes next.

a16.use can computers: Main verb cannot precede the modal.
a16.can computers use: Object cannot separate the modal from the verb.

hints: SVO: My mother + cooks + dinner.
The verb `cooks` must precede the object `dinner`.
Questions with modal `can`: Can + subject + base verb.
The modal `Can` starts the question, before the subject `you`.

a18.You can: Declarative order; `Can` must precede the subject in questions.
a18.Swim you can: The main verb cannot start a question with a modal.

hints: Present Continuous: auxiliary `is` before the -ing verb, object after the verb.
Structure: is + reading + a book.

a19.is a book reading: Object cannot come between auxiliary and -ing verb.

hints: Present Continuous: are + watching + object.
The auxiliary `are` precedes the -ing verb.

a20.watching are a film: The -ing verb cannot precede the auxiliary.
a20.are a film watching: Object cannot separate auxiliary and verb.

hints: Present Perfect: have + past participle + object.
After auxiliary `have`, the past participle `finished` comes next.

a21.finished have my homework: Past participle cannot precede the auxiliary.
a21.have my homework finished: Object cannot come between auxiliary and past participle.

hints: Negation in Present Perfect: has + not + past participle + object.
Place `not` right after `has`, before `eaten`.

a22.has eaten not breakfast: `not` must go between auxiliary and past participle.

hints: Phrasal verb `turn off` is separable: turn off + noun object.
Standard order: turn off + the light.

a23.off turn the light: The particle cannot precede the verb.
a23.turn the light off the: Extra article at the end; incorrect structure.

hints: Phrasal verb `pick up` is separable: pick up + noun object.
Standard order: pick up + the children.
Indirect object with `to`: verb + direct object + to + indirect object.
Structure: gave + a present + to her friend.

a25.gave to her friend a present: With `to`, the direct object comes before `to + indirect object`.
a25.gave a present to her friend: Correct: verb + direct object + to + indirect object.
a25.a present gave her friend to: Completely wrong order; the verb must come first.

hints: Without `to`: verb + indirect object + direct object.
Structure: gave + the students + homework.

a26.gave homework the students: Without `to`, indirect object (the students) comes before direct object (homework).
a26.the students gave homework: `the students` appears as subject; the subject is `The teacher`.

hints: With `to`: verb + direct object + to + indirect object.
Direct object `a card` comes before `to my grandma`.

a27.sent to my grandma a card: With `to`, direct object must precede `to + indirect object`.
a27.sent a card to my grandma: Correct: verb + direct object + to + indirect.
a27.a card sent to my grandma: Object cannot precede the verb in declarative sentence.

hints: Phrasal verb `put on` is separable: put on + noun object.
Standard: put on + your coat.
Frequency adverb `always` goes before the main verb.
Order: always + takes + the bus.

a29.takes always the bus: `always` should not go after the main verb.
a29.the bus always takes: Object cannot start the phrase in standard order.

hints: Frequency adverb `usually` precedes the main verb.
Order: usually + drinks + tea.

a30.drinks usually tea: `usually` should not go between verb and object.

hints: Past Simple negation: did + not + base verb + object.
Auxiliary `did` + `not` precede the main verb `visit`.

a31.did not visit the museum: Correct: did + not + visit + the museum.

hints: Present Perfect question: Have/Has + subject + past participle.
Auxiliary `Have` starts the question, before subject `you`.

a32.You have finished: Declarative order; questions need auxiliary before subject.
a32.Have you finished: Correct: Have + you + finished — question inversion.
a32.Finished have you: Past participle cannot start a question.

hints: Present Continuous question: Is/Are + subject + -ing verb + object.
Auxiliary `Is` starts the question.

a33.He is cooking dinner: Declarative order; questions need `Is` before subject.
a33.Cooking is he dinner: The -ing verb cannot start a yes/no question.

hints: Phrasal verb `write down` is separable: write down + noun object.
Particle `down` goes right after `write`, before the object.
Frequency adverb `often` goes before the main verb.
Order: often + eats + lunch.

a35.eats often lunch: `often` should not go between verb and object.

hints: Present Continuous: is + making + a cake.
Auxiliary `is` precedes the -ing verb.

a36.making is a cake: The -ing verb cannot precede the auxiliary.
a36.is a cake making: Object cannot separate auxiliary and -ing verb.

hints: When the object of a separable phrasal verb is a pronoun, it must go between the verb and the particle.
Pronoun `it` must go between `turn` and `down`: turn it down.

a37.turn down it: Pronoun objects cannot follow the particle; correct: turn it down.
a37.it turn down: The pronoun cannot precede the verb.

hints: Pronoun objects must go between the verb and particle of separable phrasal verbs.
Here `it` replaces `her jacket`: pick + it + up.

a38.pick up it: Pronoun must split the phrasal verb: pick it up.

hints: Pronoun `it` must go between the verb and particle: worked + it + out.
The pronoun replaces `the homework`.

a39.it worked out: `it` here looks like a subject, not the object of the phrasal verb.

hints: Modal + base verb + object: should + study + the material.
After `should`, the infinitive without `to` comes next.

a40.study should the material: Main verb cannot precede the modal.
a40.should the material study: Object cannot go between modal and verb.

hints: Modal negation: must + not + base verb + object.
Place `not` right after the modal `must`.

a41.must not use their phones: Correct: must + not + use + their phones.
a41.must use not their phones: `not` must go between the modal and the main verb.

hints: Modal + base verb + object: should + eat + more vegetables.
After `should`, the base verb comes directly.

a42.eat should more vegetables: Main verb cannot precede the modal.
a42.should more vegetables eat: Object cannot go between modal and verb.

hints: In Present Perfect, adverb `never` goes between the auxiliary `has` and past participle `left`.
Order: has + never + left + her keys.

a43.has left never her keys: `never` should go before the past participle, not after.
a43.has never left her keys: Correct: has + never + left + her keys.
a43.never has her keys left: In declarative sentences, auxiliary comes before the adverb.

hints: In Past Perfect, adverb `already` goes between `had` and past participle.
Order: had + already + finished + the report.

a44.had already finished the report: Correct: had + already + finished + the report.
a44.already had the report finished: Auxiliary `had` should precede the adverb in standard order.

hints: Phrasal verb `look after` is inseparable: the object must follow the complete phrasal verb.
You cannot put the object between `look` and `after`.

a45.look the children after: `look after` is inseparable; object cannot go between `look` and `after`.

hints: Phrasal verb `look for` is inseparable: object follows `for`.
Cannot separate `look` and `for` with the object.
Manner adverb `quietly` goes between auxiliary and -ing verb.
Order: were + quietly + reading + their notes.

a47.were reading quietly their notes: Adverb `quietly` is better placed before the -ing verb.

hints: Modal + base verb + object: will + order + the pasta.
After `will`, the base verb comes next.

a48.order will the pasta: Main verb cannot precede the modal.
a48.will the pasta order: Object cannot separate modal and verb.

hints: Without `to`: verb + indirect object + direct object.
Structure: gave + her brother + a book.

a49.gave a book her brother: Without `to`, indirect object comes before direct object.

hints: Without `to`: verb + indirect object + direct object.
Order: sent + all employees + an email.

a50.sent an email all employees: Without `to`, indirect object must precede direct object.
a50.all employees sent an email: `all employees` appears as subject; the subject is `The company`.

hints: Pronoun `it` must go between verb and particle: bring + it + back.
Rule: pronoun objects split separable phrasal verbs.
Frequency adverb `always` goes before the main verb.
Order: always + checks + her answers.

a52.checks always her answers: `always` should not go between verb and object.

hints: Present Perfect Continuous: have + been + -ing verb + object.
Order: have + been + cleaning + the house.

a53.have been cleaning the house: Correct: have + been + cleaning + the house.

hints: Present Perfect question: Has + subject + past participle.
Auxiliary `Has` starts the question.

a54.He has called: Declarative order; `Has` must precede the subject in questions.
a54.Has he called: Correct: Has + he + called — question inversion.
a54.Called has he: Past participle cannot start a question.

hints: Passive voice: was + past participle + prepositional phrase.
Structure: was + sent + to the manager.

a55.sent was to the manager: Auxiliary `was` must precede the past participle.
a55.was sent to the manager: Correct: was + sent + to the manager.
a55.was to the manager sent: Prepositional phrase cannot separate auxiliary and past participle.

hints: Present Perfect Passive: has + been + past participle.
Order: has + been + postponed.
Phrasal verb `come across` is inseparable: object follows the complete phrase.
Cannot place object between `came` and `across`.

a57.came an old photo across: Inseparable: object cannot go between verb and particle.

hints: Pronoun `it` must split separable phrasal verb `write down`: wrote + it + down.
Pronouns always go between verb and particle.

a58.it wrote down: `it` appears as subject, not as object of the phrasal verb.

hints: Modal passive: must + be + past participle.
Order: must + be + investigated.
Complex structure: should + have + been + past participle.
Order: should + have + been + completed.

a60.have should completed been: Modal must come first in the verb phrase.

hints: Adverb `still` goes between auxiliary `was` and -ing verb.
Order: was + still + writing + the report.

a61.was writing still the report: `still` should not go between -ing verb and object.

hints: Modal negation + phrasal verb: could + not + make up.
Place `not` right after the modal `could`.

a62.could make not up: `not` must go between modal and main verb.

hints: Adverb `just` goes between auxiliary `had` and past participle `put`.
Order: had + just + put + the children + to bed.

a63.had just put the children to bed: Correct: had + just + put + the children to bed.

hints: Separable phrasal verb `give back` with indirect object: give + object + back + to + recipient.
Order: give + the money + back + to them.

a64.give back the money to them: While possible, `give the money back to them` is more natural here.
a64.give the money back to them: Correct: verb + direct object + particle + to + indirect object.
a64.give to them back the money: Prepositional phrase cannot precede the particle and direct object.

hints: Passive infinitive: to + be + past participle.
Order: need + to be + signed.

a65.need signed to be: Passive infinitive is `to be + past participle`.
a65.to be need signed: The verb `need` must precede the passive infinitive.

hints: Future Passive: will + be + past participle.
Order: will + be + announced.
Adverb `carefully` can precede the verb: carefully + explained + direct object + to + indirect.
Order: carefully + explained + the concept + to the audience.

a67.explained carefully to the audience the: Direct object must follow the verb immediately.
a67.carefully explained the concept to the a: Correct: adverb + verb + direct object + to + indirect.

hints: Phrasal verb `run into` is inseparable: object follows `into`.
Cannot separate `ran` and `into` with the object.

a68.ran our old professor into: Inseparable: object cannot go between verb and particle.

hints: Phrasal verb `hand out` separable: hand out + direct object + to + indirect.
Order: handed out + the papers + to the class.

a69.handed out the papers to the class: Correct: phrasal verb + direct object + to + indirect.

hints: Complex structure: might + want + to + think + pronoun + over.
Pronoun `it` goes between `think` and `over` because `think over` is separable.

a70.might want to think it over: Correct: modal + want + to + phrasal verb with pronoun between verb and particle.

hints: Present Perfect Passive question: Has + subject + been + past participle.
Auxiliary `Has` starts the question.

a71.The email has been sent: Declarative order; questions need `Has` before the subject.
a71.Has the email been sent: Correct: question inversion in passive Present Perfect.
a71.Been has the email sent: `has` must come first in the verb phrase.

hints: Present Perfect Continuous + adverb: has + been + gradually + recovering + object.
Adverb `gradually` goes between `been` and the -ing verb.

a72.has gradually been recovering her streng: While possible, `has been gradually recovering` is more standard.
a72.has been gradually recovering her streng: Correct: has + been + gradually + recovering + object.

hints: Inversion with negative adverb: Never before + had + subject + past participle.
When `Never before` starts the sentence, the auxiliary precedes the subject.

a73.They had never before witnessed: Normal order; `Never before` at the start requires inversion.
a73.Never before had they witnessed: Correct: inversion after fronted negative adverb.

hints: Inversion with `No sooner`: No sooner + had + subject + past participle + than…
After `No sooner`, auxiliary `had` precedes the subject.

a74.She had no sooner closed the door: Normal order; fronted `No sooner` requires inversion.
a74.No sooner had she closed the door: Correct: No sooner + had + she + closed.

hints: Cleft sentence: It was + focused element + who/that + rest.
Structure: `It was the manager who explained`.

a75.The manager it was who explained: Disrupted cleft; `It was` must start the sentence.

hints: Pseudo-cleft: What + clause + is + focused element.
Structure: `What I really want is more time`.
Complex structure: should + have + been + adverb + past participle.
Order: should + have + been + carefully + reviewed.
Inversion with `Rarely`: Rarely + does + subject + verb.
After fronted `Rarely`, auxiliary `does` precedes the subject.
Inversion with `Not only`: Not only + did + subject + verb.
After `Not only`, auxiliary `did` precedes the subject.

a79.she did write the entire report: After `Not only`, inversion is required: did + she.
a79.did write she the entire report: Subject must go between auxiliary and main verb.

hints: Passive Progressive + adverb: is + currently + being + reviewed.
Adverb `currently` goes between `is` and `being`.
Separable phrasal verb `track down` with a long noun object: particle stays with the verb.
With long objects, the particle usually stays next to the verb.

a81.tracked the original document down: With long objects, `tracked down + object` is more natural.
a81.tracked down the original document: Correct: particle stays with verb for long objects.

hints: Cleft sentence: It is + focused element + that + clause.
Structure: `It is the budget allocation that we need to discuss`.

a82.Is it the budget allocation: Declarative cleft uses `It is` without inversion.

hints: Modal Passive + adverb: must + be + thoroughly + past participle.
Adverb `thoroughly` goes between `be` and the past participle.
Complex structure: may + have + been + adverb + past participle.
Order: may + have + been + accidentally + sent.

a84.may have accidentally sent been: `been` must precede adverb and past participle.

hints: Inversion with `Under no circumstances`: should + subject + verb.
After the fronted negative phrase, the modal precedes the subject.

a85.employees should share: Normal order; negative fronting requires inversion.
a85.should share employees: Subject must go between modal and main verb.

hints: Modal negation + inseparable phrasal verb: could + not + put up with.
Place `not` after `could`; `put up with` stays together.

a86.could not up put with: Cannot rearrange particles of inseparable phrasal verb.
a86.could not put up with: Correct: modal + not + inseparable phrasal verb.
a86.could put not up with: `not` must go after the modal, not after the main verb.

hints: Adverb + inseparable phrasal verb: completely + did away with + object.
Adverb precedes the phrasal verb; object follows all particles.

a87.did completely away with the original de: Adverb should not split inseparable phrasal verb.
a87.completely did away with the original de: Correct: adverb + inseparable phrasal verb + object.
a87.did away with completely the original de: Adverb should not go between particle and object.

hints: Inversion with `Little`: Little + did + subject + verb.
After `Little`, auxiliary `did` precedes the subject.

a88.He did little know: Normal order; `Little` at the start requires inversion.

hints: Complex structure: will + need + to be + adverb + past participle.
Order: will + need + to be + completely + renovated.

a89.will need to be completely renovated: Correct: will + need + to be + completely + renovated.
a89.will need completely to be renovated: Adverb goes before the past participle, not before `to be`.

hints: Inversion with `Only after`: Only after + clause triggers inversion in the main clause.
After `Only after signing the contract`, inversion: did + he + realize.

a90.After signing the contract only: `Only` must come at the start for inversion.
a90.Signing the contract only after: Disrupted order; `Only after` is a fixed expression.

hints: Inversion with `Not until`: Not until + clause + did + subject + verb.
After `Not until the report was published`, inversion: did + they + grasp.

a91.They did not grasp until the report was: Normal order; fronted `Not until` requires inversion.

hints: Correlative inversion: Not only + did + subject + verb, but … also.
After `Not only`, auxiliary `did` precedes subject `he`.
Complex structure: should + have + been + adverb + past participle.
Adverb `more thoroughly` goes after `been`, before `vetted`.

a93.should more thoroughly have been vetted: Adverb should not precede `have`; place after `been`.
a93.should have been more thoroughly vetted: Correct: should + have + been + more thoroughly + vetted.

hints: Pseudo-cleft: What + clause + was + focused element.
Structure: `What the committee failed to appreciate was`.
Complex: will + need + to have been + adverb + past participle.
Order: will + need + to have been + fully + implemented.
Inversion with `Scarcely`: Scarcely + had + subject + past participle.
After `Scarcely`, auxiliary `had` precedes the subject.

a96.Scarcely had the director thought: Correct: Scarcely + had + the director + thought.

hints: Cleft sentence: It was + focused element + that + clause.
Structure: `It was his meticulous attention to detail that we noticed`.
Complex: must + have + been + adverb + past participle.
Order: must + have + been + meticulously + examined.

a98.must have meticulously examined been: `been` must precede adverb and past participle.

hints: Modal negative + adverb + separable phrasal verb + pronoun: could + not + simply + brush + it + aside.
Pronoun `it` must go between `brush` and `aside`.

a99.could not brush simply it aside: Adverb `simply` should precede the verb, not follow it.
a99.could not simply brush it aside: Correct: modal + not + adverb + verb + pronoun + particle.
a99.simply could not brush aside it: Pronoun must go between verb and particle.

hints: Inversion with `Seldom` + passive: Seldom + had + subject + been + past participle.
After `Seldom`, auxiliary `had` precedes the subject.
Passive + adverb + phrasal verb: had + been + repeatedly + turned down.
Adverb `repeatedly` goes after `been`, before the phrasal verb in passive.

a101.had repeatedly been turned down by inves: While possible, `had been repeatedly turned down` is more standard.
a101.had been turned repeatedly down by inves: Adverb should not split the phrasal verb in passive.

hints: Pseudo-cleft with phrasal verb: What + clause + was + past participle.
Structure: `What she chose to bring up was known`.
Inversion with `At no point`: did + subject + verb.
After the negative phrase, auxiliary `did` precedes the subject.

a103.the company did agree to change: After `At no point`, inversion is required.

hints: Complex: ought to + have + been + adverb + past participle.
Order: ought to + have + been + formally + submitted.

a104.ought to have formally submitted been: `been` must precede adverb and past participle.

hints: Cleft with phrasal verb: It was + focused element + that + clause.
Structure: `It was her willingness to follow up on every lead`.

a105.It was her willingness to follow up on e: Correct: cleft sentence with phrasal verb.

hints: Inversion with `Hardly`: had + subject + past participle.
After `Hardly`, auxiliary `had` precedes the subject.
Complex negative: could + not + possibly + have + been + past participle.
Order: could + not + possibly + have + been + completed.

a107.could not possibly have been completed: Correct: modal + not + adverb + perfect + passive.
a107.could not have possibly completed been: `been` must precede past participle; `possibly` should precede `have`.

hints: Adverb + inseparable phrasal verb: carefully + going over + object.
Adverb `carefully` precedes the phrasal verb `going over`.

a108.going carefully over every clause: Adverb should not split inseparable phrasal verb.
a108.carefully going over every clause: Correct: adverb + inseparable phrasal verb + object.
a108.going over carefully every clause: Adverb should not go between particle and object.
